---
title: "The value in teaching is not the content, it's the teacher"
subtitle: "Naarm (Melbourne)"
date: "2026-07-10"
author: "Nicholas Tierney"
institute: "Statistical Consultant<br>nipaluna, lutruwita (Hobart, Tasmania)"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: [blood, extra.scss]
    incremental: true
    slide-number: c
    show-slide-number: all
    footer: "njtierney.github.io/talk-teacher-over-content/"
execute: 
  echo: false
  warning: false
  cache: true
  freeze: true
---

```{r}
#| label: library
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(colorspace)
library(naniar)
library(visdat)
```


```{r}
#| label: source-r-files
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| output: false
lapply(list.files(here::here("R"), full.names = TRUE), source)
```

```{r}
#| label: helpers
#| include: false

theme_set(
  theme_grey(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent")
  )
)

# **ni**ck's **pa**lette
nipa <- list(red = "#c03018",
             orange = "#f0a800",
             green = "#609048",
             purple = "#484878",
             light_purple = "#A3A3BB",
             light_green = "#AFC7A3",
             light_orange = "#F7D37F",
             light_red = "#DF978B",
             pale_purple = "#ECECF1",
             pale_green = "#D7E3D1",
             pale_orange = "#FBE9BF",
             pale_red = "#EFCBC4")

```

# Short course materials should be online and free

## {background-iframe="https://qmd4sci.njtierney.com/" background-interactive="true"}

## {background-iframe="https://intro2rpkgs.njtierney.com/" background-interactive="true"}

## Short course materials should be online and free

:::{.highlight-last}

- People aren't paying for your content
- They are paying for your **time**
- **Time**  >>> content
- Your course should demonstrate:
  - The ideas are important and worthwhile to learn
  - It is possible to learn it
- The online materials continue to support that learning

:::

## Audience poll

- Who teaches: 0.5...1...2-3 day(s), semester courses?
- Who teaches short courses?
- Whose material is online for free?
- This talk is aimed at **short course materials**.
  - Not "accredited courses": e.g., CPR, actuarials, etc.

## **Time** >>> content

- time, not content, is the bottleneck for learning
- The learner carves out time: protects it from distraction
- The learner gets YOUR time as the teacher
- They get to ask an expert, clarify misunderstandings
  - "How did you do that?"
  - "Why this way over XXX?"
  - "Is that the same as YYY?"
  
## Course ideas are important and worthwhile

> I think the two most important messages that people can get from a short course are:

> a) the material is important and worthwhile to learn (even if it's challenging), and 
> b) it's possible to learn it!

-- Hadley Wickham - [from a github gist in 2015](https://gist.github.com/hadley/37c8078eb9d46b5dac7e)

## The online materials continue to support that learning

- No searching through emails to find PDFs of slides
- Course materials are a living document that will be up to date
- This material adds to the narrative, provides help in the future
- Deepens your own understanding of the topic: built-in professional development

## How can "free" be sustainable?

- Courses aren't a primary product/source of income
- I view courses and teaching as a way to get people in front of you
- Demonstrates your expertise - show people what you know
  - This is "Free advertising"
  - These could be future clients

## What materials are "free"?

- Slides
- Written notes
- Text books (that you have written)
- Quiz questions/answers

:::{.notes}
This is essentially an extension of ideas that I have seen from peers in my community. There is a growing movement to make your textbooks free and available online.
:::

## Free learning isn't a new idea

- [Statistical Rethinking on Youtube]("https://www.youtube.com/@rmcelreath/playlists")
- [khan academy](https://www.khanacademy.org/)
- [edX](https://www.edx.org/)
- [coursera](https://www.coursera.org/)
- [Harvard's CS50](https://pll.harvard.edu/course/cs50-introduction-computer-science)
- [MIT's linear algebra course](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-06-linear-algebra-spring-2010/)

## Examples in R community

- Yihui Xie: ["write a book with Chapman & Hall"](https://yihui.org/en/2018/08/bookdown-crc/)
- Jenny Bryan: ["happygitwithr"](https://happygitwithr.com/), [What They Forgot to Teach You About R](https://rstats.wtf/), ["stat545"](https://stat545.com/)
- Hadley Wickham: ["Advanced R"](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/), ["Mastering Shiny"](https://mastering-shiny.org/), ["R Packages"](https://r-pkgs.org/)
- [Forecasting Principles and Practice](https://otexts.com/fpp3/)
- Miles McBain: [git for scientists](https://milesmcbain.github.io/git_4_sci/)

## What do people pay for then?

-  I am not saying: "Deliver your courses for free"
-  I am saying: People pay for their time in your course
-  I am saying: give people an option
- If they want to put in the extra work, use your materials as a resource, to learn the thing, great
- If they want to see you "in person" (or on screen?), then they can pay for that
- But putting the course material up online kind of only really serves to help you

:::{.notes}
- If students/users are given a packet of slides or teaching materials, then if they return to them in the following weeks/months/years, then are they really going to be kept up to date?
- How do you find your teaching materials again? Is it in an email? What if you don't have access to that email address again?
- Make your teaching material accessible - put it on github, on your website, on zenodo. Put it online.
- Congratulations, your teaching resource has now transformed into 

:::

## What should people pay for

- Video recording of the course. Captures their experience.
  - Video privacy: safe space for private questions/sharing 
- Online exercises
- Grading or assessment
- Your time
- Hard copy of book

## Example: qmd4sci

- Built upon "Rmarkdown for scientists"
- [11 contributions from external people - I did not request or ask for this help](https://github.com/njtierney/rmd4sci/pulls?q=is%3Apr+is%3Aclosed+-author%3Anjtierney+)
- github repo: [njtierney/qmd4sci](https://github.com/njtierney/qmd4sci)
- exercises: [njtierney/qmd4sci-materials](https://github.com/njtierney/qmd4sci-materials)
- website: [qmd4sci.njtierney.com](https://qmd4sci.njtierney.com)
- rmd4sci: 127 stars on github
- qmd4sci: 67 stars on github
- 

## How to: put a course online

- Make a bookdown/quarto book
- Add an appropriate license [CC BY-NC-4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/)
  - You can share/adapt
  - You must attribute
  - You cannot use the material for commercial purposes
- Put it on github
- Have it render when you make changes
- Deliver the course in-person, or online
- You don't need to share "course notes" or slides with your students
- They just get everything from the online book

## On "free advertising"

- And, yes, you better bet I'm taking this chance to advertise my teaching material
- In fact, I'm advertising my training.njtierney.com courses!

## Take homes

- Short course materials should be online and free
  - People aren't paying for your content
  - They are paying for your **time**
  - **Time**  >>> content
  - Your course should demonstrate:
    - The ideas are important and worthwhile to learn
    - It is possible to learn it
  - The online materials continue to support that learning


# Thanks

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width = "40%"}

- Maëlle Salmon
- Miles McBain
- David Borg
- Committee for bursary

:::

::: {.column width = "40%"}

- Hadley Wickham
- Jenny Bryran
- Yihui Xie
- Di Cook

:::

::::

# Resources

- Quarto
- bookdown
- slidecraft

# Colophon

- Slides made using [quarto](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto)
- Colours taken + modified from [lorikeet theme from ochRe](https://github.com/ropenscilabs/ochRe)
[njtierney/njt-talks](github.com/njtierney/njt-talks)
<!-- - Header font is **Josefin Sans** -->
<!-- - Body text font is **Montserrat** -->
<!-- - Code font is **Fira Mono** -->
<!-- - template available:  -->

# Learning more

- **talk link** [talk link]()

- **github** `@njtierney`

- **email** nicholas.tierney@gmail.com

# **End.**

# Extras

# ❤️ Hiking! `njt.micro.blog`

![](images/pct-collage.jpg){background-size="75%"}

# End

# Cutting room floor


(Demo training.njtierney.com?)


## Dave Borg

One of the most useful things in a workshop is seeing the process - it convvinces you that the thing is worth learning. 

these workshops help improve your  mental model - which allows you to improve how you learn a thing, as you have more nodes.

how much time are you affording to sit down and solve things - your coding resilience.

## How this is different for university courses

- Universities might not want your teaching content online for free - copyright can be challenging here
- The learning at a university course happens consistently over a time period of like 13 weeks. You are practicing the skills in that time. Of course, it would still be useful if the materials are always there and easy to find.
- Mostly you are then practicing using the skills during the university semester.
- However, a short course often follows a pattern of a student taking time off to learn the material, then they might not have a direct opportunity to 


::: {.notes}

To build a sustainable consulting practice, I think you need a genuine value-add to the wider community. I am not convinced writing a post such as "10 things you need to do now to improve your data visualisations" cuts it. Teaching does. And more, I think you should share your course materials for free.

In this talk I will argue teaching and openly sharing your materials is a sustainable business model. The value learners pay for when they attend a course is not the slides or the code, those should be freely available online. The value they get, which they are paying for, is time with the instructor, and the commitment they make to carving out space to learn something new.

Developing free and open content compounds over time. It creates a living document you can refer to and update. It demonstrates your expertise not by telling people you know things, but by showing them. It surfaces your work to learners who would never have found you otherwise, and those learners become the pipeline for future consulting and collaboration. It also deepens your own understanding of the topic. So, you get professional development built in.

I will discuss a case study in R training: _Quarto for Scientists_ to illustrate how this works in practice. I hope to leave attendees with the conviction that creating useful, openly available learning content is not a distraction from building a consulting practice, but is a highly effective way to build, and sustain one.

:::

## **Time**: watch and learn

- Contents do not substitute watching someone work

## Ideas so far:

- Your course has enticed people to attend
- You convince them the ideas are worthwhile learning, and that they can learn them


## Sustainable consulting

- We need to put our knowledge back out into the community
- Not a LinkedIn™ post: 
  - "The 10 types of clustering that all data scientists need to know. Let's dive in:"
- Not a "free webinar"
- Your teaching materials
- Online, free. Forever.

:::{.notes}
- Why do we "need" to give back?
- Why is this sustainable?
:::

:::{.notes}
I will discuss a case study in R training: _Quarto for Scientists_ to illustrate how this works in practice. 
:::

:::{.notes}
I hope to leave attendees with the conviction that creating useful, openly available learning content is not a distraction from building a consulting practice, but is a highly effective way to build, and sustain one.
:::

## Can you make some parts "not free"

- I think there is a case for the following to be made 'paid':
  - Online recordings of the course material
  - Video content of the course
